chapter_summary: The first chapter of the Prasna Upanishad begins with six seekers approaching the sage Pippalada with questions about the ultimate reality. After a year of disciplined practice, Kabandhi Katyayana asks about the origin of creatures. Pippalada explains that Prajapati, desiring offspring, performed austerity and created the pair Rayi (matter) and Prana (life force), identifying Rayi with the moon and Prana with the sun, and describing how the sun distributes life force through its rays.

story.title: The Quest for the Ultimate Reality
story.arc: Six seekers approach sage Pippalada; after a year of discipline, Kabandhi asks about the origin of creatures; Pippalada explains creation through the pair Rayi and Prana.
story.timeline_note: The events occur after a year of disciplined studentship under Pippalada.
story.comic_potential: The scene of six earnest seekers with firewood in hand approaching a sage has a formal, ritualistic quality that could be depicted with solemnity.

teachings.teaching: The ultimate reality (Brahman) can be approached through disciplined practice (tapas, brahmacharya, shraddha) and humble inquiry under a qualified teacher.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the necessity of preparatory practices (kriya) and guru-seva before receiving higher knowledge.

teachings.teaching: Prajapati (Hiranyagarbha) created the dual principles Rayi (matter) and Prana (life force) as the basis for all creatures.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga sees this as the cosmic duality of consciousness (Prana) and its object (Rayi), which the yogi must harmonize through pranayama.

teachings.teaching: The sun (Aditya) is identified with Prana (life force) and the moon with Rayi (matter); the sun distributes prana through its rays to all directions.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this correlates with the solar plexus and lunar channels; the yogi's practice of breath control aligns the microcosmic prana with the macrocosmic sun.

chapter_summary: This chapter of the Prasna Upanishad, with Shankara's commentary, explores the nature of Prajapati (the year) as the union of Rayi (matter, moon) and Prana (spirit, sun). It describes the two paths: the southern path (pitryana) leading to the moon and rebirth for those who perform rituals, and the northern path (devayana) leading to the sun and liberation for those who practice tapas, brahmacharya, shraddha, and vidya. The chapter also discusses the microcosmic representations of Prajapati in the month (dark and bright fortnights) and day (night and day), with ethical implications for sexual activity.

story.title: The Two Paths: Rayi and Prana
story.arc: Explanatory discourse on the nature of Prajapati and the paths to rebirth or liberation.
story.comic_potential: Low; abstract concepts and ritual paths.

teachings.teaching: The year (Prajapati) has two paths: the southern path (pitryana) leads to the moon and rebirth for those who perform rituals; the northern path (devayana) leads to the sun and liberation for those who practice tapas, brahmacharya, shraddha, and vidya.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this teaching emphasizes the importance of inner discipline (tapas) and knowledge (vidya) over mere ritual action. The northern path corresponds to the yogic ascent of prana through the sushumna, leading to liberation.

teachings.teaching: The month (Prajapati) has two halves: the dark fortnight is Rayi (matter), the bright fortnight is Prana (spirit). Wise sages perform rituals in the bright half, others in the dark.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the interplay of tamas (darkness) and sattva (light) in the practitioner's life. Kriya-Yoga aims to transform the dark, material tendencies into luminous spiritual energy.

teachings.teaching: The day (Prajapati) has day as Prana and night as Rayi. Those who have sex during the day waste their prana; sex at night (in season) is considered brahmacharya.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes conservation of vital energy (ojas). This teaching aligns with the practice of brahmacharya to preserve prana for spiritual ascent.

teachings.teaching: Vaisvanara (Prana) is the all-formed fire that rises daily, absorbing all directions. He is the sun with a thousand rays, manifesting in various forms as the prana of beings.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as the cosmic prana that the yogi experiences in kriya meditation as the inner light and heat rising through the chakras.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Prasna Upanishad (with Shankara Bhashya) discusses the nature of Prajapati as food and the process of creation through the union of day and night, leading to the origin of beings. It then explores the hierarchy of deities (elements and senses) and establishes Prana (the vital force) as the supreme among them, demonstrated by a parable where Prana's departure causes all others to fail.

story.title: The Supremacy of Prana
story.timeline_note: After the first question on creation, the second question delves into the hierarchy of deities supporting the body.
story.comic_potential: The parable of Prana leaving and all other faculties failing is dramatic and could be visualized as a body collapsing.

teachings.teaching: Prajapati is food, and from food comes seed, from which creatures are born.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the transformation of gross matter into subtle life force through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: Those who follow the Prajapati vow (ritual union during the season) produce offspring and attain the lunar world.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the householder's path of balancing material and spiritual duties, leading to gradual purification.

teachings.teaching: Those free from crookedness, falsehood, and deceit attain the pure solar world (viraja brahmaloka).
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's emphasis on inner purity and truthfulness as prerequisites for higher yogic states.

teachings.teaching: Prana is the supreme among the deities (elements and senses); when it departs, all others depart; when it stays, they stay.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would highlight this as the key to Kriya-Yoga: controlling Prana through breath regulation to master the mind and senses.
